speaker: interviewer
text: December 7, 1995. Gerda, we left off on the last tape with the separation of your mother and yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I w-- I was with some of my friends. I think I, I really was not totally aware of exactly what happened at that moment. There was a great deal of, of confusion and chaos. Our mothers were put into another enclosure. It was near the railroad station where the circus used to come with a very large metal type stuff. And trucks appeared. And something rather curious and, for me, something that I could never really resolve in my happened. There was a man by the name of Merin. Merin was someone who worked for the authorities, not a person to be much admired.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the story had it that he had a little daughter whom he adored. And the Germans said that if he cooperates with them, he and the child are going to be saved. Of course, that was not the case. But in any event, he cooperated. And Merin was-- as a matter of fact, a little earlier my mother said to me, people say this is Merin. Go and ask him what, what is happening? Where we going? And I remember I went up and asked him. And I asked him, where are we going? He sort of laughed and didn't give me an answer. So I knew who he was. He wearing a black raincoat. I can still see him. He was a very, very slight, small man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the trucks appeared, and my friends-- all young girls-- and we were loaded on those trucks. And I heard my mother's voice from that enclosure, and, and she was calling something. And at this point, I realized that I was being taken away from my mother, so I jumped off the truck. And Merin appeared, and he took me bodily and threw me back on the truck. And he said, you are too young to die. And I yelled out, I hate you. I hate you. Something like that. But what a strange thing. He who helped the murder of my mother saved my life. And I was never been able to, to resolve that terrible feeling within me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I heard my mother's voice again, and she said, where to? And I called back-- it was in German. I said, [GERMAN], I don't know. And she called, be strong. And I heard her voice like an echo. It was [GERMAN]. Then to trucks were set into motion, and ironically, the church bells started ringing, and the sound broke through. It was raining all day. It was a miserable day, very hot and oppressive because it was June, end of June. And, and that was that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were loaded up on a train, and we were taken to a place called Sosnowiec, which was a large Jewish community. And it contained a Durchgangslager, which was a transit camp. I was with my friend, particularly my friend Ilse, a childhood friend of mine, was a very artistic girl. She had won some piano competition. She was sent to Vienna to study at the Conservatory of Music. And we became to each other the only family we had because some people had sisters. I never had a sister, and Ilse sort of became my sister. And make a long story short, in, in Sosnowiec we were in the Durchgangslager. I think it's the first time when we really came face to face what camps were like because as we were given the first food there in sort of battered tin bowls and looked very unappetizing, I saw there were some other girls who had just come back. And told us that they had come back from the camps in which they work. They were on their way to Auschwitz because they were either maimed or contracted tuberculosis. And I remember particularly one girl with enormous eyes. She must have been about my age. She looked at the food, and I asked her if, if she wanted. I didn't want to eat that. You know it was sand at the bottom and horrible things. Little did I realize what was in store for me and how much that food would have meant later. I remember I gave her that food. And I-- she said she was going-- they were going to Auschwitz. She knew it that in a few days, she would be no more. That was sort of the first confrontation with, with what there was. Anyway, we, we were sent two days later to-- actually it was like a slave market. Because our names and our ages and the places we hailed from were registered, and industrialists from all over Germany would come to buy slaves. I mean not literally buy them, but they paid us in 10 German reichsmarks for the processing of, of our papers. And since we came from Bielitz, which was known for textile factories, as fate would have it that particular day a man came from [GERMAN], which was one of the largest textile factories in Germany. When he saw the roster that girls from Bielitz were there, for some reason he thought we might have more dexterity in weaving or whatever. He bought us for that camp, and this is where we went. And as it turned out, it was a one of the, quote unquote 'best camps in Germany.' It was a new camp. We were the first 50 girls. We were 50 girls that went to that camp, mostly from-- some from Bielitz, but mostly from Czechoslovakia because there were very few girls-- maybe a handful, eight to 10 girls-- from Bielitz. And the rest had come from Czechoslovakia. We were all German-speaking. German men were in the army. The German women were given responsible positions. And they needed slave laborers who could understand the language so they could teach us. And we went through very rigorous sort of, you know, things. You had to tie so many weaving knots in minutes. They stood over you, you know, with a watch. And of course, sometimes, you were nervous and there's people couldn't make it. But most of us did, and we were taught weaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: An interesting thing happened in that camp-- The name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was called Bolkenhain. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The person-- the first person we encountered in Bolkenhain who was going to be in charge of us was a woman that was dressed all in black. She looked like a bulldog. She had the most forbidding face I'd ever seen and a voice that resembled no human voice. She barked like a dog. I mean, I thought this must be the end of it. We'll never survive here. Well, she turned out to be the most decent human being. She was probably chosen for her work by her demeanor, but actually she was a decent and very caring person. Nobody was sent to Auschwitz. We were with her for a little over a year. She, she really gave one hope, you know. And I think she particularly pinned a lie to the lips of all of those Germans that they didn't have a choice. There were plenty of choices.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Frau Kugler. I've been looking for her. Now when HBO did the film, they searched for her high and low. We all left-- the few of us who survived left depositions that if she is found, you know, that we would like to show our gratitude. Unfortunately, she was not found.

speaker: interviewee
text: She saved my life one day. I was ill. I was running a fever, probably had I don't know what, and two other girls. And we were permitted to stay in our bunks and not go to the factory. And suddenly Lindner appeared. He was one of those fearful SS people who used to come down without warning, you know? And if he found anybody that was in the camp ill, went immediately to Auschwitz and he was known for his cruelty. And Frau Kugler came running in and she said, I'll take her to, to the factory when she found out that he was there. I had my skiing boots. My father made me wear my skiing shoes in June. You didn't argue with your father. I remember it was a hot day in June, and he said, I want you to wear your ski boots. And I said, why? He said, I want you to wear them today, and I wore them for three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those boots really saved my life on the death march. But also, you know, I was still growing, and we didn't have any-- we had the same clothes for all those years. And of course, in skiing shoes, you know, in those days you wore two, three pairs of socks. So even when my feet were growing there was still enough room in my boots.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wore them all those years. And in them also I had hidden the pictures of my parents and my brother, which are now in my book. So she stooped down and she helped me, you know, buckle my, my boots. She took the two other girls and she literally dragged us to the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I couldn't see anywhere. We got to our loom. She had apparently worked on looms before. She set my looms in motion and she said, pull yourself together. It's life or death today. And I remember the looms, it was like-- [INAUDIBLE]. I was so sick. But when Lindner passed all my looms were in motion and, and she saved my life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So that was Bolkenhain. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think there is one thing that, at least to my mind, has never been sufficiently-- not-- not-- not given enough credit. And that was the type of support system that existed in the camps-- a friendship, the sharing, the caring. Again, you know, it depends from camp to camp. I was in camp only with girls, and it was such an incredible support system. People were just, just marvelous to each other. Not everybody was saintly, but it was really one for all and all for one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some examples of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember for instance, one time-- that was in another camp, but not in Bolkenhain, but Bolkenhain-- you know, [GERMAN] had three camps, and I was in all three of them. And this particular thing happened in another camp. We were standing in the enclosure. We were not with Frau Kugler anymore at that point. But you had to stand appell, you know, roll call, for a very long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly bread sailed over the barbed wire. And we knew who threw the bread, and we knew who had it. And everybody was beaten that day. Nobody would give the girl away who, who had the bread. I mean, this was the sort of thing, you know? In Bolkenhain, were you able to do anything? What did you girls do when you had any free time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have an awful lot of free time. But when we did, I know people would-- I wrote a play. It was a Hanukkah play, and that's something that is still very important to me. My friend Ilse and I played two grandmothers in that play. And we predicted, of course, a brilliant future for everyone. The stage was on a clearing between the bunks on the one-legged [? bob. ?] Then our two granddaughters came and listened to us for a while. And then in a loud stage whisper they said, come let's talk about our boyfriends. You know how old people exaggerate. And so we were 16 and 18 at that time. We seemed to have caught the very core of our hope that one day we live in a world where Jewish grandmothers will live and Jewish grandchildren will live. But best of all, our grandchildren will not believe our tales of the past. There will be such a climate. And we made a menorah out of the evening meal of potatoes, which meant of course you didn't eat, you know. We carved the little menorah. We didn't have candles, of course, but we felt that, symbolically, we would light the flame of hope. And we wrote a song [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about that. And I had an incredible fulfillment of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, my friend Ilse did not survive. She died in my arms on a wet meadow in Czechoslovakia. She once gave me a raspberry which she had found in the gutter, carried all day in her pocket and presented me that night in a leaf that she plucked through the barbed wire. One single dust-covered raspberry, her entire possession.

speaker: interviewee
text: She also gave me my life by making me promise her when she died a week before the war ended to go on for one more week. But Ilse and I played in that role. And we had-- I had fulfillment of that many, many years later when I was in Israel. And when the person who played my granddaughter called from the lobby of the hotel and said, Gerda, we are here-- same words that she said when we went on stage-- and when my friends came out of the elevator, they carried a small menorah in memory of the one which we made in camp that night. My friend's name is Fanny Steinhardt Goldschmidt in Israel, who played Ilse's granddaughter-- who played my granddaughter, I'm sorry. The girl who played Ilse's granddaughter, just as Ilse, is not alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did that in Bolkenhain. We-- you know, whoever remembered a play or a particular book or something would, would tell it. You know, it was not what a lot of people think. And of course, I can only talk about the camps in which I was. And I will say it was easier because, you know, it was not a murder camp. Even though people were sent, but nobody was sent from Bolkenhain.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked very hard, but we still had, had some food. They had to give us some sustenance in order to be able to work. And Frau Kugler was there. So-- and we were in this camp for a little over a year, which made it to the early fall of 1943, when actually, you know, some of the most brutal transports started in Europe. Because in that time nobody went then to camp anymore, into any working camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: The physical conditions in Bolkenhain. What was your barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all stayed in one large barrack. [DRINKING] We had bunks, straw-filled sort of things. We had showers, cold ones, cold. We didn't have any warm water. We still got some food, thanks to Frau Kugler. There was some physical punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes people were beaten by somebody in the factory and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten in Bolkenhain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Bolkenhain, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any women give birth or any women pregnant in Bolkenhain?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were all young girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you menstruate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a hard thing because you had no supplies, you know? We had to find little pieces of paper and some things from under the loom of which you fashion things. And finally later on, we were given some saltpeter or something, you know, that you would not-- and I never ate when that was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was one of the butt of jokes. I still hear sometimes from friends because they say, what you mean? I said, there's one thing I wanted if I survive. I wanted to have children. And everybody kidded me with that. But it was sort of an obsession that I had. You remained in Bolkenhain for one year.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a little over a year. A little over a year. From there, we went to Merzdorf. Merzdorf was a horrible, horrible camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from one camp to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: By truck. Actually, what happened was because [GERMAN] had several factories, when the raw material or whatever was depleted in Bolkenhain, you know, things were getting worse for-- and war effort, you know, was also that. So they sent us to different camps. And I was-- Ilse and I and a bunch of girls were sent to Merzdorf. Some of our other friends were send to Landeshut. Merzdorf, to me, was the most horrible camp that I was in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not far. I don't know how many kilometers away, but I would say probably a two-hour truck ride or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. It was all in Germany. All the camps that I was were in Germany. And there we were housed and very high up, perhaps the fifth or sixth floor. We were locked up night there. I always thought it's going to burn. It was either terribly hot or cold, mostly stifling hot. We, we were given terrible work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the work?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the things where we were in silos. And they were doing flax, from which linen was extracted, you know? And trucks would come and throw those bundles. And they came, and so some people simply were trapped under the bundles if you didn't catch them fast enough. I remember one day we had some women prisoners-- murderers and things like that-- who came, and to them it was a day of punishment to be sent to work on flax for one day. And this is where we worked. And the thing would scratch with the bundles would drop on you, you know, with the precision of I don't know how many seconds. And you had to catch them as you are continuously swaying because, you know, the ground was giving away from under your feet. It was soft bundles, and it was tremendous silos. I don't know how many stories high. And then another day we would go and open the flax in the swamp-- the swamp, and the flax had to be broken open because there were linen threads in that. And of course the swamp was infested with mosquitoes. And since we were all scratched from the flax, you know, the mosquitoes feasted on our blood. And at night-- and I was particularly-- there was one German there who had it in for me. One day he tempted me, asked me, you know, if I would like some warm soup or something to eat. No, he asked me if I had enough to eat. So I said I was eating in camp. I wasn't going to say I don't get enough to eat because it would have been accusing them of not feeding me. And to say I had enough to eat wasn't true. I was always hungry. So I thought he had other designs, and I was scared, scared stiff. I played dumb.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he singled me out for working at night unloading coal, huge coal trains, you know, and during the day to flax. And in the afternoon, first in the swamp or in the silos. And that was [INAUDIBLE]. And this is when I one night I figured that's it. I'm just going to jump. This is what I mentioned in my-- and my father thinking back. And then one day a miraculous thing happened. As we were working in the swamp, they came and asked for such and such a number. You know, we were all had numbers. And actually it was Ilse's number, But Ilse saw what was happening, so she said it was my number, see? I got back to camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there stood the director from Bolkenhain. And then Ilse came running and she threw herself at his feet and she said, I want to go with my sister. So he said, well, you both should be on the list because we both worked on looms. Make a long story short, we, we were sent to Landeshut, which was a sister camp of, of Bolkenhain. And in Landeshut we, again, we worked on the spinning mills. And so I went to Landeshut. I was in Merzdorf only a very short time, mercifully.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was a 886, but I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- did you have to wear your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's a funny thing. I don't recall that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, it's funny. I don't recall if we wore it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- you said Franz Kugler--

speaker: interviewee
text: Frau Kugler, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was not in this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in the camp again when we came to Landeshut.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Merzdorf--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Merzdorf, she was not. In Merzdorf, we had a cruel, stupid young girl. She was wearing a ring on every finger, and her, her great enjoyment was to ride in a-- in a little child's wagon and have the girls pull her like horses. And she had a whip over that. I mean, the mentality of a total moron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any punishments in Merzdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. There was punishment, beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you punished in Merzdorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it surround--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was punishment enough, you know, in the type of work. Another thing we did in Merzdorf, we were building something. And they were throwing bricks, and you had to catch them very fast. And I remember bricks landed on my hands and my feet and, you know, it-- you just never-- unless you were in the rhythm of things, you couldn't catch up with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it surrounded by barbed wire at all, this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, escaping was not that difficult-- not difficult. You could run away any time you wanted to. But where do you go? And the other thing, of course, was that you knew-- we heard of a boy's camp where apparently some boys escaped or killed a guard or something. That they were all marched out and every third one was shot. I mean, you didn't take this type of responsibility for your friends. And furthermore, if you run away, where do you go?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were working in the factory, or in these different camps so far, Bolkenhain and Landeshut, did you have any contact at all with the civilian population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they worked next to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know? Did you talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course they knew. Talk-- you didn't talk. You never dared open your mouth to tell anybody because what do you do? You know, you can't tell them life is wonderful. And you certainly didn't dare complain. You didn't know to whom you were talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: But by and large, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very few were kind, you know? There were some instances, some girls said somebody left him a little food or something. But, but later on, we worked only on the night shift where the Germans worked the day shift. We worked for about eight months, I think, on the night shift continuously. We didn't have any, any contact. And frankly, I was scared stiff of them. During this time, were you aware of what was going on--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the world?

speaker: interviewer
text: --In the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing we knew is, you know, they broadcast-- you know, they had radio receiver things all over the factories. And the news would come forth and blast forth with, of course, announcing victories. And it was always preceded by a little song, which was started out like [GERMAN], we are going against England. Then you knew. And then it said, [GERMAN]. And that meant that they were announcing a victory where they were going. So we always knew. And that already started early. I remember when France fell. One of the hardest things was when Paris fell. I was still at home, of course, at the time. But that is about all the information that we had. Absolutely no idea about the progress of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to receive any correspondences at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- certainly in Bolkenhain. I had my uncle in Turkey, and they urged me to write to him. And later on I realized why. Because he sent packages with things which were not obtainable in Germany. And by some miracle, the packages were always came almost totally depleted, you know? But I was told to acknowledge it. So I knew what they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else did you hear from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard from something like a boyfriend. He was still in a camp in Bielitz. We heard from him, got packages from him. My nana would write occasionally. And of course my letter to my father had come back in Bolkenhain still. That was one of the toughest blows.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard from my brother the last time I was in Bolkenhain, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] just when the Germans took this part of the Russian. I had one more letter from my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Merzdorf how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of months. I'm really not quite sure. I should have looked it up in my book. I knew it much more accurately before. And then I went to Landeshut.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the conditions in Merzdorf as compared to Bolkenhain you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sleeping conditions--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everything about it was awful. You know, that the person in charge of us was this young girl. And then there was this other one, which was an absolute beast. She used to come in the morning with, with a whip. It was just dreadful. And then it was almost like heaven when we got to Landeshut. We were housed in a-- in a stable, but Frau Kugler was there, and some of the other girls that I knew. And it was a wonderful reunion to come back there. But we only worked on the night shift, so my entire stay in Land-- in Landeshut, we worked on the night shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the conditions in Landeshut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very difficult. The conditions were deteriorating. And the work was very hard because we were weaving silk with, I don't know, what could have been 20,000 threads. Mostly during the night. It was illuminated. It was very bright. And, you know, if a thread broke, that was considered-- if you didn't work properly to not be able to repair it, you fell behind in the quota. And, and of course, you know, food was getting less and less. But, but I was still with my friends there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to have any plays, like what you did in Bolkenhain? Could you do that in Landeshut?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- you know, We had much less free time, if you could call it free time. Also, we mostly slept during the day because we worked at night, and things were much, much tougher there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the support system, did it remain as strong?

speaker: interviewee
text: Support system, oh yes, yes. That remained strong throughout.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there was a boy's camp not far. Did you have any contact at all with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. You mean in Landeshut? And the boy whom I knew had come to Landeshut. That's a whole long story in itself. But we did have some, some contact. We heard some horrible things that are going on in boy's camps-- much, much worse than what we experienced.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were in Landeshut?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there until May 8, 1944.
